You are doing error analysis for a harness-optimization task. Read-only investigation, report findings only.

Compare two rollouts of the SAME task at two harness versions:
- Baseline (bare harness): /workspace/rollouts/corporate-ma_review-governance-diligence_scenario-01/iter0/
- Edited harness (added a "complete issue census + cross-reference every document" method to the system prompt): /workspace/rollouts/corporate-ma_review-governance-diligence_scenario-01/iter1/

iter0 scored 0.96 (failed only C-037, C-039). iter1 REGRESSED to 0.81 (failed C-001, C-002, C-003, C-008, C-009, C-021, C-022, C-030, C-037, C-038).

In each iterN dir: task.md (rubric with criteria text), output/ (deliverables — read .md directly; for .docx/.xlsx use `python3 -c` to unzip and extract text from word/document.xml or sharedStrings.xml), grade.json (failed_criteria), transcript.jsonl (the run; the final assistant turns show the deliverable).

ANSWER THESE QUESTIONS precisely:
1. What do the newly-failed criteria C-001, C-002, C-003, C-008, C-009, C-021, C-022, C-030 actually require (quote the criterion text)?
2. Looking at the iter0 vs iter1 deliverables, WHY did each newly-failed criterion fail in iter1 but pass in iter0? Categorize each as one of: (a) FABRICATION/over-coverage (agent flagged a non-issue or distractor), (b) DROPPED a required fact/issue that iter0 had (dilution — agent spread attention thin), (c) RESTRUCTURED the deliverable so the required content is buried/missing, (d) FORMAT/length problem, (e) noise/unclear.
3. Did iter1's deliverable get LONGER or more elaborate than iter0's? Roughly how many issues/sections did each raise?
4. One-paragraph bottom line: what is the dominant MECHANISM by which the "issue census + cross-reference" prose caused this regression?

Be concise and concrete. Quote specific deliverable text where it proves your point. Do NOT write any files.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

7	DATE: October 28, 2024
8	RE: Governance Diligence — Proposed Acquisition of Terravox Systems, Inc. by Hargrove Capital Partners III, LP
9	
10	I. Executive Summary
11	This memorandum summarizes our review of the governance documents of Terravox Systems, Inc. (“Terravox” or the “Company”) in connection with the proposed acquisition of 100% of the Company’s outstanding equity by Hargrove Capital Partners III, LP (“Hargrove” or “Buyer”), structured as a reverse triangular merger at an enterprise value of $485 million (the “Transaction”). We reviewed the Company’s Third Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation (the “Restated Certificate”), Amended and Restated Bylaws (the “Bylaws”), Stockholders’ Agreement, Voting Agreement, Registration Rights Agreement (the “RRA”), and Board minutes covering January 2023 through September 2024 (the “Board Minutes”).
12	The central finding of this diligence is that Stonemill Capital Partners Fund VI, LP (“Stonemill”) holds a hard, charter-level blocking right over the Transaction. As the holder of 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, Stonemill alone controls the Series C separate-class protective provision under Section 4.3.6(b)(i) of the Restated Certificate, which requires the affirmative vote or written consent of holders of at least 66⅔% of the Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class, to effect any “Liquidation Event.” The reverse triangular merger is unambiguously a “Liquidation Event.” Because Stonemill holds all 7,800,000 outstanding Series C shares, the 66⅔% threshold (≥5,200,000 shares) cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent. This blocking right cannot be cured by the drag-along mechanism in the Stockholders’ Agreement with any certainty under Delaware law, as discussed in Section IV.
13	A second, time-sensitive finding concerns the Company’s letter of intent with SensorLink Technologies, Inc. (“SensorLink”). The SensorLink LOI contains an automatic-renewal exclusivity provision, and the Board Minutes reflect uncertainty as to whether a formal termination notice was delivered before the September 8, 2024 expiration date. If no notice was sent, a live exclusivity obligation may be running in favor of SensorLink and could conflict directly with the Company’s ability to sign definitive documentation with Hargrove. This requires immediate confirmation and remediation.

57	Bottom line for the client. There is no clean, litigation-free path to closing the Transaction over Stonemill’s objection. We recommend that the deal strategy proceed on parallel tracks: (a) pursue Stonemill’s affirmative consent as the primary path, while (b) preserving and pressure-testing the drag-along as a fallback, supported by a focused Delaware-law opinion on the drag-along/protective-provision interplay. The SensorLink exclusivity question should be resolved immediately and should not await the balance of diligence.
58	
59	II. Transaction Overview and Capitalization
60	Transaction structure. Hargrove will form a wholly-owned acquisition subsidiary (“Merger Sub”) that will merge with and into Terravox, with Terravox surviving as a wholly-owned subsidiary of Hargrove. All outstanding equity interests of Terravox (Common Stock and all series of Preferred Stock) will be cancelled and converted into the right to receive cash merger consideration. The structure is a reverse triangular merger under Section 251 of the Delaware General Corporation Law (the “DGCL”).
61	Deal economics. Enterprise value: $485,000,000. Outstanding indebtedness under the Clearfield National Bank term loan: approximately $12,000,000. Implied equity value: approximately $473,000,000. Based on FY2023 revenue of $127.3 million and Adjusted EBITDA of $31.8 million, the implied multiples are approximately 3.81x revenue and 15.25x EBITDA. On a fully diluted basis of approximately 33,900,000 shares, the blended implied per-share equity value is approximately $13.95, subject to the Preferred Stock liquidation waterfall.
62	Liquidation waterfall. The total aggregate liquidation preference of the outstanding Preferred Stock is $206,400,000 (Series C: 7,800,000 × $18.50 = $144,300,000; Series B: 5,200,000 × $9.25 = $48,100,000; Series A: 3,500,000 × $4.00 = $14,000,000). Because the implied equity value ($473 million) substantially exceeds the aggregate liquidation preference ($206.4 million), all Preferred holders are “in the money” on an as-converted basis. The Board Minutes (Sept. 25, 2024) confirm Meridian’s preliminary view that participation on an as-converted basis would be economically favorable for all preferred holders, meaning the waterfall is expected to collapse to an as-converted distribution. This is economically significant for the consent analysis: Stonemill’s economic upside on an as-converted basis (approximately 23% of the equity) substantially exceeds its liquidation preference alone, which may inform its negotiating posture but does not eliminate its governance leverage.
63	Capitalization (as of November 3, 2021, per Stockholders’ Agreement Exhibit B; assumed current unless noted).

98	III. Area 1 — Change-of-Control Consent Requirements
99	To consummate the reverse triangular merger, the following consents and approvals must be obtained. We have mapped each requirement to its source provision, identified the threshold and the parties whose consent is required, and assessed the current status.
100	A. Board approval (DGCL § 251(b); Bylaws Art. III)
101	The Board must adopt a resolution approving the merger agreement and the merger (DGCL § 251(b)). Under Bylaws Section 3.7, a majority of the total number of directors then in office (4 of 7) constitutes a quorum, and the act of a majority of directors present at a quorum is the act of the Board. The Board has authorized management to negotiate definitive documentation (Board Minutes, Sept. 25, 2024, approved 6-1, Trask dissenting), but final Board approval of the signed merger agreement has not yet been obtained. Status: Not yet obtained (pending definitive documentation).
102	B. Stockholder approval — DGCL § 251(c)
103	DGCL § 251(c) requires the approval of holders of a majority of the outstanding shares of stock entitled to vote (the Company’s Restated Certificate and the protective provisions cause the Preferred to vote on an as-converted basis with the Common as a single class on this matter). This is a majority of the approximately 33,900,000 fully diluted shares, i.e., more than 16,950,000 shares. Status: Not yet obtained. This vote can be taken by written consent in lieu of a meeting under DGCL § 228 and Restated Certificate Article VI, Section 6.2 / Bylaws Section 2.6.
104	C. All-Preferred protective provision — Restated Certificate § 4.3.6(a)(vi)
105	So long as any Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least a majority (greater than 50%) of the then-outstanding Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis (Section 4.3.6(a)(vi)). The Restated Certificate specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of more than 8,250,000 shares of Preferred (based on 16,500,000 Preferred shares outstanding: 3,500,000 Series A; 5,200,000 Series B; 7,800,000 Series C).
106	Can this be satisfied without Stonemill? Yes. Ridgeline (3,500,000) + Northbridge (5,200,000) = 8,700,000 shares, which exceeds the 8,250,000 threshold. Stonemill’s 7,800,000 shares represent 47.3% of the Preferred — a large block, but not a blocking position on this particular vote. Status: Not yet obtained; achievable without Stonemill.
107	D. Series C separate-class protective provision — Restated Certificate § 4.3.6(b)(i) — CRITICAL
108	So long as any Series C Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class (Section 4.3.6(b)(i)). This consent is “separate from and in addition to” the all-Preferred consent under Section 4.3.6(a). The Restated Certificate specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of at least 5,200,000 shares of Series C (based on 7,800,000 Series C shares outstanding).
109	Stonemill holds 7,800,000 shares of Series C — i.e., 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock. The 66⅔% threshold (≥5,200,000 shares) therefore cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent. This is a hard, charter-level blocking right. The Board Minutes (Sept. 25, 2024) confirm that outside counsel Rourke advised the Board of this requirement and that Julian Trask stated on the record that “the Series C separate class vote… cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent.” Status: Not yet obtained; cannot be obtained without Stonemill.
110	E. Drag-along (Stockholders’ Agreement Art. 3) — conditional
111	If the drag-along is invoked and its trigger conditions are met, all Restricted Stockholders (including Stonemill) would be obligated to vote in favor of the Sale of the Company. Whether the drag-along can be used to satisfy the Series C separate-class protective provision is the central legal question addressed in Section IV. Status: Not yet invoked.
112	F. DGCL § 251(f) “short-form” alternative — not available
113	We considered whether the merger could be structured to avoid a stockholder vote under DGCL § 251(f) (no stockholder vote required where, among other things, no amendment to the certificate of incorporation is made in the merger and either the acquirer owns 90% of the target or certain other conditions are met). This is not available here: Hargrove does not currently own any Terravox stock, and the merger will cancel all outstanding equity for consideration. A stockholder vote (or written consent) is required.
114	G. Other protective provisions implicated
115	We note for completeness that several other Section 4.3.6(a) protective provisions are not directly triggered by the merger itself but are relevant to related actions: Section 4.3.6(a)(i) (charter/bylaw amendments adversely affecting Preferred — relevant if the merger agreement requires a charter amendment); Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) (increasing/decreasing authorized shares); and Section 4.3.6(a)(vii) (increasing/decreasing Board size). The merger as structured (cancellation of all equity for cash) does not require a charter amendment, so these are not independently triggered, but we should confirm that the merger agreement does not contemplate any pre-closing charter amendment that would independently require Preferred consent.

119	IV. Area 2 — Drag-Along Analysis
120	The Stockholders’ Agreement (Article 3) contains drag-along rights that, if properly triggered, obligate all Restricted Stockholders to vote in favor of, and participate in, a Sale of the Company. This section (i) runs the numbers on whether the drag-along can be triggered without Stonemill, and (ii) addresses the critical legal question of whether the drag-along can override the charter-level Series C protective provision.
121	A. The three-pronged trigger (Section 3.1(a))
122	A “Drag-Along Approval” requires the satisfaction of all three of the following conditions (Section 3.1(b) confirms that fewer than all three is insufficient):
123	Common majority (as-converted): holders of at least a majority of the outstanding shares of Common Stock, calculated on an as-converted basis (treating Preferred as converted) — i.e., more than 16,950,000 of the 33,900,000 fully diluted shares;
124	Preferred majority (as-converted, single class): holders of at least a majority of the outstanding Preferred Stock on an as-converted basis — i.e., more than 8,250,000 of the 16,500,000 Preferred shares; and
125	Investor Stockholder approval: at least two (2) of the three (3) Investor Stockholders.

143	Prongs (2) and (3) are satisfied without Stonemill. Prong (1) is the obstacle: the Founders, Ridgeline, and Northbridge together hold 16,000,000 fully diluted shares (47.2%), which falls just short of the 16,950,000 majority threshold. To satisfy prong (1) without Stonemill, the Company would need to secure the support of at least approximately 950,000 additional shares from the “various common holders / option pool” block (10,100,000 shares, ~29.8% of the cap table).
144	Assessment. Prong (1) is marginally achievable without Stonemill but is not guaranteed. The 10,100,000-share block consists of employee option/RSU holders and other common holders, many of whom are not parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement (the drag-along binds only “Restricted Stockholders,” i.e., parties to the agreement, but the trigger threshold in prong (1) counts all outstanding Common on an as-converted basis, including non-party holders). In a cash-out merger at a premium, employee holders would generally be supportive, but (a) a meaningful portion of outstanding options appear to be out-of-the-money (see Section IX), reducing the economic incentive for those holders to vote, and (b) we have not yet confirmed which option/common holders are parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement via joinder. We recommend confirming the joinder status of the option/common holders and modeling the realistic vote count for prong (1).
145	If prong (1) can be satisfied (with the support of sufficient option/common holders), then a Drag-Along Approval can be obtained without Stonemill’s affirmative support, and the drag-along would compel Stonemill — as a Restricted Stockholder — to vote its 7,800,000 Series C shares in favor of the Sale of the Company (Section 3.1(a), obligations A–D) pursuant to the irrevocable proxy granted in Section 3.3.
146	C. Can the drag-along override the Series C separate-class protective provision? — THE KEY LEGAL QUESTION
147	This is the decisive question for the Transaction. Even if the drag-along is triggered without Stonemill and compels Stonemill to vote its Series C in favor, the question is whether that compelled vote satisfies the charter’s separate-class protective provision in Section 4.3.6(b)(i), which requires the “affirmative vote or written consent” of 66⅔% of the Series C voting as a separate class.
148	Textual arguments that the drag-along CAN satisfy the protective provision:
149	The charter is satisfied by a vote, not by a discretionary veto. Section 4.3.6(b) is satisfied by “the prior written consent or affirmative vote” of 66⅔% of the Series C. It does not require a voluntary or uncoerced consent; it requires a vote or consent of the requisite threshold. If the drag-along compels Stonemill (100% of the Series C) to vote in favor, that is an “affirmative vote” of 100% of the Series C, which exceeds the 66⅔% threshold. On this reading, the charter requirement is satisfied, not waived or amended — the protective provision is given effect through a compelled vote.
150	Stonemill contractually agreed to be dragged. By executing the Stockholders’ Agreement, Stonemill agreed to the drag-along with a trigger that expressly permits approval by only two of the three Investor Stockholders (Section 3.1(a)(iii)) — i.e., Stonemill agreed to be dragged without its own affirmative support, so long as the other thresholds are met. The drag-along includes an irrevocable proxy “coupled with an interest” (Section 3.3) to vote Stonemill’s shares “in favor of… any matter required to be approved in connection with such Sale of the Company” — which would include the Series C separate-class vote. Article 3 is further protected from amendment without Stonemill’s consent (Section 7.4), reflecting that Stonemill knowingly accepted these terms.
151	Delaware enforces stockholder voting agreements. DGCL § 218(b) validates agreements among stockholders as to the manner in which they will vote. Delaware courts routinely enforce voting agreements, drag-along provisions, and irrevocable proxies coupled with an interest as binding contracts.
152	Arguments that the drag-along CANNOT override the protective provision:
153	The protective provision is a charter (class) right of superior dignity. Section 4.3.6(b) is embedded in the certificate of incorporation — the supreme corporate document — and grants the Series C, as a class, a separate-class consent right over any Liquidation Event. A stockholders’ agreement is a contract among stockholders; it cannot amend or override the certificate of incorporation except through the charter’s own amendment procedures (which themselves require Series C consent under Section 4.3.6(b)(ii)). Allowing a drag-along to compel the sole Series C holder to vote away its class veto arguably defeats the protective provision’s purpose and effectively amends the charter by contract — something Delaware law does not permit.
154	The protective provision protects the class, not just the holder. The Series C protective provision is framed as a class right (voting “as a separate class”). A drag-along that compels the only class member to vote in favor arguably eliminates the class protection that the charter was specifically negotiated to provide. A court could view this as an impermissible circumvention of the charter’s class-voting structure.
155	Uncertainty and litigation risk. There is no Delaware Supreme Court decision squarely holding that a stockholders’-agreement drag-along can compel a vote that satisfies a charter-level separate-class protective provision over the objecting holder’s opposition. While the textual arguments above are colorable, the question is genuinely unsettled, and Stonemill would have a credible basis to challenge enforcement.
156	Our assessment. This is a close and unsettled question of Delaware law. The textual argument that the drag-along can compel a vote that satisfies (rather than overrides) the protective provision is reasonably strong, particularly given that Stonemill expressly agreed to a 2-of-3-investor trigger and granted an irrevocable proxy. However, we cannot advise the client with confidence that a Delaware court would enforce the drag-along to override Stonemill’s separate-class veto over its objection. Stonemill would almost certainly litigate, seeking injunctive relief to prevent closing on the basis that the Series C separate-class consent was not validly obtained. Even if Hargrove ultimately prevailed, the litigation would introduce material closing delay, cost, and deal uncertainty — outcomes that are anathema to a private-equity buyer with a fixed fund life and a Q1 2025 target close.
157	Recommendation. Treat Stonemill’s affirmative consent as the primary and preferred path to closing. Preserve the drag-along as a negotiating lever and a fallback: the existence of a credible (if contested) alternative path to closing is itself valuable in negotiations, because it shifts Stonemill’s calculus from “I can block” toward “I may be dragged unless I negotiate.” We recommend obtaining a focused Delaware-law opinion (or at minimum a thorough memorandum) on the drag-along/protective-provision interplay before relying on the drag-along in negotiations or as a closing mechanism. We also recommend that the merger agreement be drafted to permit closing upon either (a) Stonemill’s affirmative Series C consent, or (b) a court-enforced drag-along, with appropriate closing-condition drafting.
158	D. Drag-along terms affecting consideration (Section 3.2)
159	If the drag-along is invoked, Section 3.2(a) provides that each Restricted Stockholder receives the same form and amount of consideration per share (as-converted), except that liquidation preferences apply and consideration is distributed “in accordance with the liquidation waterfall provisions of the Restated Certificate as if the Sale of the Company were a Liquidation Event.” This means the drag-along does not override the liquidation waterfall — Preferred holders still receive their preference-ordered distribution. Given that all Preferred is in the money on an as-converted basis, this is consistent with an as-converted distribution. Section 3.2(d) provides that no dragged stockholder can be required to give non-competes or to indemnify in excess of consideration received — useful protections that should be reflected in the merger agreement’s stockholder representative / indemnity provisions.

161	V. Area 3 — Impediments to Closing a Reverse Triangular Merger
162	A. Does the “Liquidation Event” definition capture a reverse triangular merger?
163	Yes, unambiguously. Section 4.3.2(d)(i) of the Restated Certificate defines a “Liquidation Event” to include “any merger, consolidation, or other business combination of the Corporation with or into another entity (whether or not the Corporation is the surviving entity), other than a merger… in which the stockholders of the Corporation immediately prior to such transaction continue to hold at least fifty percent (50%) of the voting power of the surviving or resulting entity immediately following such transaction.”
164	In the reverse triangular merger, Merger Sub merges into Terravox and Terravox survives — but as a wholly-owned subsidiary of Hargrove. Immediately following the merger, Terravox’s former stockholders hold 0% of the voting power of the surviving entity (Hargrove, through Merger Sub, owns 100%). The 50% continuation exception therefore does not apply, and the merger is a Liquidation Event under Section 4.3.2(d)(i). It is independently captured by Section 4.3.2(d)(iii) (a transaction resulting in the transfer of more than 50% of the Company’s voting power to any person), since Hargrove will hold 100% post-closing.
165	The “Sale of the Company” definitions in the Stockholders’ Agreement (Section 1.1) and the Voting Agreement (Section 1) likewise capture the merger: both define a Sale of the Company to include any merger in which the Company’s stockholders do not retain a majority of the voting power of the surviving or resulting entity, and any transaction transferring more than 50% of the outstanding voting power. The reverse triangular merger satisfies both. No structural ambiguity exists as to whether the merger triggers the Liquidation Event / Sale of the Company definitions.
166	B. Structural features and advantages of the reverse triangular merger
167	The reverse triangular merger structure is generally favorable for this Transaction. Because Terravox survives as the legal entity (rather than disappearing into Merger Sub), the Company’s contracts, licenses, permits, and regulatory approvals remain in place at the surviving entity without requiring consents or novations (subject, of course, to any change-of-control provisions in those contracts — a separate diligence workstream). This is the principal advantage of the reverse triangular structure over a forward merger. The structure does not, by itself, create any governance impediment beyond the consent requirements analyzed in Section III.
168	C. The one structural ambiguity: the RRA’s “Deemed Liquidation Event”
169	The RRA terminates upon “a Deemed Liquidation Event” (Section 9(c)), but the RRA does not define “Deemed Liquidation Event” and does not cross-reference the Restated Certificate’s “Liquidation Event” definition. This is the one structural/definitional ambiguity affecting the Transaction and is addressed in detail in Sections VIII and XII. In summary: the merger almost certainly is intended to be a “Deemed Liquidation Event” (the term is conventionally used to mean a change-of-control transaction treated as a liquidation for preferred purposes), but the RRA’s failure to define or cross-reference the term creates a non-trivial risk that registration rights could be argued to survive the merger. This should be cured by an express termination/waiver of the RRA at closing.
170	D. Indebtedness and change-of-control provisions
171	The $12 million Clearfield National Bank term loan is secured by substantially all of the Company’s assets. The loan agreement almost certainly contains change-of-control and/or default-on-acceleration provisions that will be triggered by the merger. Repayment or refinancing of this debt at or before closing is a condition to the Transaction (the $485 million enterprise value reflects $12 million of debt, yielding $473 million equity value). We have not yet reviewed the Clearfield loan agreement; it should be obtained and reviewed for change-of-control triggers, prepayment penalties, and any lender consent requirements. This is a separate (finance) workstream but is flagged here because a lender consent could be an additional closing condition.
172	
173	VI. Area 4 — Transfer Restrictions
174	A. ROFR and co-sale mechanics (Stockholders’ Agreement Art. 2)
175	The Stockholders’ Agreement imposes a general restriction on transfers (Section 2.1): no Restricted Stockholder may transfer Shares except (a) in compliance with Article 2, (b) pursuant to a Permitted Transfer (Section 2.7), or (c) in connection with a Sale of the Company consummated in accordance with Article 3. The right of first refusal (Section 2.2) gives the Company 30 days to purchase shares subject to a bona fide third-party offer, followed by a 20-day stockholder ROFR period. The co-sale right (Section 2.3) allows Investor Stockholders to participate in a proposed transfer if the Company and other stockholders do not purchase all the shares. Tag-along rights (Section 2.4) apply to Founder transfers of more than 5% of the outstanding shares.
176	B. Does the merger trigger ROFR or co-sale?
177	In principle, no — but the carve-out is conditional, and the condition must be carefully managed. Transfers “in connection with a Sale of the Company” are exempted from the transfer restrictions in two places: Section 2.1(c) (a general carve-out) and Section 2.7(e) (a Permitted Transfer). However, both carve-outs are expressly conditioned on the Sale of the Company being “consummated in accordance with Article 3” — i.e., the drag-along article.
178	This creates a potential trap. If the Transaction is approved and consummated by stockholder consent without formally invoking the drag-along (for example, if the Company simply solicits the DGCL § 251 vote and the protective-provision consents directly, without a Drag-Along Approval), then a litigant could argue that the Sale was not “consummated in accordance with Article 3” and that the ROFR/co-sale carve-outs do not apply. While the better view is that a company-wide merger for uniform consideration is not the kind of individual stockholder sale that the ROFR/co-sale provisions were designed to govern, the conditional language (“consummated in accordance with Article 3”) creates arguable ambiguity.
179	Recommendation. Structure the Transaction so that the Sale of the Company is expressly effected “in accordance with Article 3” of the Stockholders’ Agreement — i.e., obtain a Drag-Along Approval and invoke the drag-along as the mechanism for the stockholder vote. This cleanly brings the Transaction within the Section 2.1(c)/2.7(e) carve-outs and eliminates ROFR/co-sale ambiguity. As a belt-and-suspenders measure, obtain written ROFR/co-sale waivers from all Restricted Stockholders at signing or closing. Note that invoking the drag-along for this purpose is consistent with — and does not conflict with — the analysis in Section IV; the drag-along can be invoked to secure the stockholder vote and the ROFR/co-sale carve-out even if the Series C protective provision is separately satisfied by Stonemill’s affirmative consent.
180	C. Termination of the Stockholders’ Agreement
181	Section 6.1(b) provides that the Stockholders’ Agreement terminates upon “a Sale of the Company (as defined in Section 1.1).” The merger is a Sale of the Company. Accordingly, the Stockholders’ Agreement terminates at closing, and upon termination all transfer restrictions, ROFR, co-sale, tag-along, and drag-along rights cease (Section 6.3), and the Company must remove the related legends. Status: Will terminate at closing, provided the merger is a Sale of the Company (which it is). The survival of Article 7 (Miscellaneous) and pre-termination breach liability (Section 6.2) should be noted but do not impede closing.

210	Section 2.3 provides that a designated director may be removed only upon the written request of the designating party, and vacancies are filled by the designating party. Section 2.4 requires that each Board committee include at least one Investor Stockholder designee (unless otherwise agreed by the Investors). Section 3.1 obligates all parties to vote to maintain this composition.
211	B. Termination of the Voting Agreement upon the merger
212	Section 7.1 provides that the Voting Agreement terminates upon the earliest of: (a) an IPO; (b) the consummation of a Sale of the Company (as defined in Section 1); (c) written consent of the Company, a majority of the Founder Common, and a majority of each series of Preferred held by parties (voting as separate classes); or (d) November 3, 2026 (the fifth anniversary — the “sunset”).
213	The merger is a Sale of the Company under the Voting Agreement’s definition (Section 1), so the Voting Agreement terminates at closing under Section 7.1(b). Post-closing, Terravox is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Hargrove; Hargrove, as sole stockholder, will control the composition of the Terravox board. The designation rights, voting obligations, and committee-composition requirements all fall away at closing.
214	C. Stonemill’s board observer right — does it survive?
215	Section 4.1 grants Stonemill the right to appoint a non-voting board observer for so long as Stonemill holds at least 1,000,000 shares of Series C (or Common issued upon conversion). The observer is entitled to attend all Board and committee meetings and to receive all materials provided to directors (subject to attorney-client privilege and conflict-of-interest exclusions under Section 4.2).
216	The observer right does not survive the merger. Two independent reasons: (1) the Voting Agreement terminates at closing under Section 7.1(b) (Sale of the Company); and (2) even if the Voting Agreement did not terminate, the observer right is conditioned on Stonemill holding ≥1,000,000 Series C (or conversion Common), and at closing Stonemill’s Series C is cancelled for cash — so the condition fails and the right expires under Section 7.3. Post-closing, Hargrove controls the Terravox board and there is no continuing observer right.
217	D. The sunset risk (Moderate)
218	If, contrary to expectation, the merger were held not to be a “Sale of the Company” under the Voting Agreement (e.g., due to a definitional dispute), the Voting Agreement would not terminate at closing and would continue in force until the November 3, 2026 sunset. In that scenario, the designation rights and voting obligations would technically remain in force. However, the practical impact is limited: post-closing, the former stockholders (Founders and Investors) no longer hold shares (cashed out), so their voting obligations are moot, and the individual designation/observer rights expire under Section 7.3 when the minimum-share thresholds are no longer met. The residual risk is low but non-zero, and it reinforces the importance of the definitional-consistency analysis in Section VIII. We recommend that the merger agreement include an express termination/waiver of the Voting Agreement at closing to eliminate any sunset-related lingering risk.
219	
220	VIII. Area 6 — Cross-Document Definitional Inconsistencies
221	We compared the change-of-control defined terms across the five governance documents. The principal findings are below.
222	A. “Liquidation Event” (Restated Certificate § 4.3.2(d)) vs. “Sale of the Company” (Stockholders’ Agreement § 1.1; Voting Agreement § 1)
223	These terms are substantially consistent in capturing the reverse triangular merger, but differ in two respects:
224	Threshold for the merger continuation exception. The Restated Certificate’s “Liquidation Event” (Section 4.3.2(d)(i)) excludes a merger in which the Company’s stockholders continue to hold “at least fifty percent (50%)” of the voting power — i.e., a merger in which stockholders retain exactly 50% is not a Liquidation Event. The “Sale of the Company” definitions in the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement exclude a merger in which stockholders “do not retain a majority” of the voting power — i.e., a merger in which stockholders retain exactly 50% is a Sale of the Company (because 50% is not a majority). This is a subtle inconsistency that could produce different outcomes in the rare case of a merger in which stockholders retain exactly 50%. It does not affect the Hargrove Transaction (stockholders retain 0%), but it should be noted and, if practicable, conformed in any post-closing cleanup.
225	Scope. The “Liquidation Event” definition includes a fourth sub-part (Section 4.3.2(d)(iv): voluntary or involuntary liquidation, dissolution, or winding up) that the “Sale of the Company” definitions do not expressly include. This is a minor scope difference; a liquidation/dissolution would not typically be a “Sale of the Company” but would be a “Liquidation Event.” Again, not relevant to the merger.

9	I. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
10	This memorandum summarizes our review of the governance documents of Terravox Systems, Inc. (“Terravox” or the “Company”) in connection with the proposed acquisition of 100% of the Company’s outstanding equity by Hargrove Capital Partners III, LP (“Hargrove” or “Buyer”), structured as a reverse triangular merger at an enterprise value of $485 million (the “Transaction”). We reviewed the Company’s Third Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation (the “Restated Certificate” or “Charter”), Amended and Restated Bylaws (the “Bylaws”), Stockholders’ Agreement, Voting Agreement, Registration Rights Agreement (the “RRA”), and Board minutes covering January 2023 through September 2024.
11	The central finding of this diligence is that Stonemill Capital Partners Fund VI, LP (“Stonemill”) holds a hard, charter-level blocking right over the Transaction that cannot be reliably overcome through the governance documents alone. Specifically, Section 4.3.6(b)(i) of the Restated Certificate requires the affirmative consent of holders of at least sixty-six and two-thirds percent (66⅔%) of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class, to effect any “Liquidation Event” — a threshold that, because Stonemill holds 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock (7,800,000 shares), cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent. The reverse triangular merger squarely constitutes a “Liquidation Event” under Section 4.3.2(d) of the Charter. This is the decisive leverage point in the deal and the throughline of this memorandum.
12	Key conclusions:
13	No viable path to closing over Stonemill’s objection via the governance documents. The Series C separate-class protective provision is a non-waivable charter right. The drag-along in the Stockholders’ Agreement, even if triggered, most likely cannot override this charter protective provision under Delaware law (see ODN Holding Corp. 25, LLC v. Knowlton, Del. Ch. 2011). Accordingly, the Transaction cannot be consummated without Stonemill’s consent, and the negotiation strategy must be built around obtaining it.
14	The all-Preferred protective provision CAN be satisfied without Stonemill. Section 4.3.6(a)(vi) requires the consent of holders of a majority of the Preferred Stock (voting as a single class on an as-converted basis), a threshold of more than 8,250,000 of the 16,500,000 outstanding Preferred shares. Northbridge (5,200,000) and Ridgeline (3,500,000) together hold 8,700,000 shares, sufficient to satisfy this threshold without Stonemill. This narrows Stonemill’s blocking position to the Series C separate-class vote alone.
15	Stonemill’s economic motivation to block is strong. At $485 million enterprise value (approximately $473 million equity value), the Series C liquidation preference ($144.3 million) consumes a substantial portion of the equity proceeds, and Stonemill would receive essentially its Original Issue Price back with no return, while Series B, Series A, and Common holders capture the upside. This economic structure explains Stonemill’s resistance and its likely demand for a higher allocation of consideration or a side arrangement.
16	A time-sensitive exclusivity issue with SensorLink Technologies may impede signing. The SensorLink LOI contains an automatic-renewal exclusivity provision; the Company’s CEO was unable to confirm whether a termination notice was sent before the September 8, 2024 expiration. If the exclusivity is still live, it could conflict with the Company’s ability to sign a definitive agreement with Hargrove. This requires immediate attention.
17	The $12 million Clearfield National Bank term loan appears to have been incurred without the Preferred Stockholder consent required by Section 4.3.6(a)(v) of the Charter. The Board minutes reflect only director approval, not the separate stockholder consent the Charter requires for indebtedness exceeding $5 million. This is a governance defect that Stonemill may invoke as leverage.
18	The Special Committee is not fully independent. Sandra Okafor, Northbridge’s board designee, serves on the three-member Special Committee overseeing the sale process. Her presence undermines the Committee’s independence and its cleansing effect, creating a process vulnerability that Stonemill may exploit.
19	Critical document gaps remain. We have not received the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, individual award agreements, the Clearfield loan documents, the SensorLink LOI, or any stockholder consent records. Several conclusions in this memorandum are necessarily preliminary pending production of these items.

66	III. AREA 1 — CHANGE-OF-CONTROL CONSENT REQUIREMENTS
67	To consummate the reverse triangular merger, the following consents and approvals must be obtained. We have mapped each requirement across the Charter, the Stockholders’ Agreement, the Voting Agreement, the RRA, and the DGCL.
68	A. DGCL Requirements (Section 251)
69	Under Section 251 of the Delaware General Corporation Law (the “DGCL”), a merger requires (i) approval of the merger agreement by the board of directors of each constituent corporation and (ii) approval by the stockholders of the surviving corporation, unless an exemption applies. The Section 251(f) exemption from stockholder approval is not available here because all outstanding shares of Terravox will be cancelled and converted into the right to receive cash — the shares do not “remain outstanding as the same share” as required by Section 251(f)(2). Accordingly, stockholder approval under Section 251(d) is required: the affirmative vote of a majority of the outstanding shares of stock entitled to vote. This is consistent with the analysis of Company counsel (Nathaniel Rourke) reflected in the September 25, 2024 Board minutes.
70	B. Charter Protective Provisions
71	1. All-Preferred protective provision (Section 4.3.6(a)(vi)). So long as any Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least a majority (greater than 50%) of the then-outstanding Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis. The Charter specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of more than 8,250,000 shares of Preferred Stock (based on 16,500,000 shares outstanding).
72	The reverse triangular merger is a “Liquidation Event” under Section 4.3.2(d)(i) (a merger in which the Company’s stockholders do not continue to hold at least 50% of the voting power of the surviving entity) and under Section 4.3.2(d)(iii) (a transaction resulting in the transfer of more than 50% of the voting power).
73	This threshold CAN be satisfied without Stonemill. Northbridge (5,200,000 shares) and Ridgeline (3,500,000 shares) together hold 8,700,000 shares, which exceeds the 8,250,000-share threshold. Stonemill’s 7,800,000 shares are not required.
74	2. Series C separate-class protective provision (Section 4.3.6(b)(i)). So long as any Series C Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class. The Charter specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of at least 5,200,000 shares of Series C (based on 7,800,000 shares outstanding).
75	This threshold CANNOT be satisfied without Stonemill. Stonemill holds 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock (7,800,000 shares). Julian Trask confirmed this on the record at the September 25, 2024 Board meeting, stating that “the Series C separate class vote … cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent.”
76	The Section 4.3.6(b) consent is “separate from and in addition to” the Section 4.3.6(a) consent; both must be independently obtained.
77	C. Stockholders’ Agreement — Drag-Along (Article 3)
78	The Stockholders’ Agreement contains drag-along rights that, if the “Drag-Along Approval” thresholds are met, would obligate all stockholder parties (including Stonemill) to vote in favor of a Sale of the Company. The drag-along is analyzed in detail in Section IV (Area 2). For purposes of the consent map: the drag-along is not itself a “consent” required to consummate the merger, but it is a mechanism that, if validly triggered and enforceable, could compel dissenting stockholders to vote in favor. As discussed in Section IV, the drag-along most likely cannot override the Series C separate-class protective provision.
79	D. Voting Agreement
80	The Voting Agreement’s voting obligations (Section 3) relate to board composition, not to the merger itself. 89	IV. AREA 2 — DRAG-ALONG ANALYSIS
90	A. The Three-Pronged Trigger
91	Section 3.1(a) of the Stockholders’ Agreement provides that a “Drag-Along Approval” is obtained only upon the satisfaction of all three of the following conditions:
92	Common vote: holders of at least a majority of the outstanding shares of Common Stock (calculated on an as-converted to Common Stock basis, treating each share of Preferred Stock as converted) (Section 3.1(a)(i));
93	Preferred vote: holders of at least a majority of the outstanding shares of Preferred Stock (calculated on an as-converted basis, voting together as a single class) (Section 3.1(a)(ii)); and
94	Investor vote: at least two (2) of the three (3) Investor Stockholders (Section 3.1(a)(iii)).
95	Section 3.1(b) confirms that all three conditions must be satisfied; fewer than all three does not constitute a Drag-Along Approval.
96	B. Can the Drag-Along Be Triggered Without Stonemill?
97	Condition (ii) — Preferred vote: The threshold is a majority of the 16,500,000 outstanding Preferred shares (as-converted), i.e., more than 8,250,000 shares. Without Stonemill, Northbridge (5,200,000) and Ridgeline (3,500,000) together hold 8,700,000 shares — sufficient to satisfy this condition. Satisfiable without Stonemill.
98	Condition (iii) — Investor vote: At least two of the three Investor Stockholders (Ridgeline, Northbridge, Stonemill) must approve. Without Stonemill, both Ridgeline and Northbridge must approve. Satisfiable without Stonemill (assuming both Ridgeline and Northbridge consent, which is consistent with their expressed support in the Board minutes).
99	Condition (i) — Common vote: This condition is the principal uncertainty. The provision requires a majority of the outstanding Common Stock “calculated on an as-converted to Common Stock basis, treating each share of Preferred Stock as having been converted.” Read naturally, this is a majority of the fully diluted as-converted base. On a fully diluted basis of approximately 33,900,000 shares, a majority requires more than approximately 16,950,000 shares. The non-Stonemill parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement (the Founders, Ridgeline, and Northbridge) collectively hold 16,000,000 as-converted shares (Founders 7,300,000 + Ridgeline 3,500,000 + Northbridge 5,200,000) — approximately 950,000 shares short of the threshold. Satisfaction of this condition without Stonemill would therefore require the support of additional Common holders who are not parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement (a portion of the 10,100,000 shares held by “Various Common Holders / Option Pool”). The availability of that support cannot be confirmed without the complete capitalization table and clarification of whether unexercised options and unvested RSUs are counted as “outstanding shares of Common Stock.” Uncertain — likely requires non-party Common holder support.
100	Preliminary conclusion on trigger: Conditions (ii) and (iii) can be satisfied without Stonemill. Condition (i) is uncertain and may require support from non-party Common holders. Even if all three conditions are satisfied, however, the more fundamental question is whether the drag-along can override the Charter’s Series C protective provision, addressed below.
101	C. Can the Drag-Along Override the Charter Protective Provisions?
102	This is the critical legal question. If the drag-along is validly triggered, Section 3.1(a) obligates each Restricted Stockholder — including Stonemill — to vote all of its Shares (including its Series C Preferred Stock) in favor of the Sale of the Company. Section 3.3 reinforces this with an irrevocable proxy granted to the holders constituting the Drag-Along Approval. If Stonemill were compelled to vote 100% of its Series C in favor, the 66⅔% Series C separate-class threshold would be satisfied.
103	The difficulty is that the Series C separate-class vote is a protective provision embedded in the Charter — the supreme governance document of the Company. Under Delaware law, a stockholders’ agreement is a contract among stockholders and cannot amend or override the certificate of incorporation. The leading authority is ODN Holding Corp. 25, LLC v. Knowlton, 2011 WL 6153080 (Del. Ch. Dec. 2, 2011), in which the Court of Chancery held that a drag-along provision could not be used to circumvent the class-voting protections of the certificate of incorporation. The court reasoned that the certificate of incorporation is the supreme governing document and that a stockholders’ agreement cannot be employed to override its protective provisions or to force a transaction that violates a charter class-vote requirement.
104	There is a colorable counter-argument: the drag-along does not “amend” the Charter but rather compels a stockholder to vote in a particular way, and if the compelled vote satisfies the Charter threshold, no Charter provision is violated. On this view, Stonemill contractually agreed to be dragged and must vote accordingly. The irrevocable proxy in Section 3.3 strengthens the enforcement argument. However, this argument is in tension with the ODN reasoning and has not been reliably endorsed by Delaware courts as a means to override a separate-class protective provision to which the protected holder objects.
105	Assessment. This is a genuinely contested question under Delaware law. The weight of authority and the ODN line of cases suggest that the drag-along most likely cannot override the Series C separate-class protective provision. Relying on the drag-along to close over Stonemill’s objection would expose the Transaction to significant litigation risk, including a likely injunction motion by Stonemill seeking to enjoin the closing on the ground that the Series C separate-class consent was not validly obtained. The outcome of such litigation is uncertain and would likely delay or derail the Transaction.
106	Recommendation. Do not rely on the drag-along as a primary path to closing over Stonemill’s objection. Treat the Series C separate-class vote as a hard blocking right requiring Stonemill’s affirmative consent. The drag-along may be retained as a secondary negotiating lever and a potential litigation fallback, but it should not be the cornerstone of the closing strategy.
107	D. Drag-Along Terms Affecting Consideration
108	Section 3.2(a) provides that each Restricted Stockholder receives the same form and amount of consideration per share (as-converted), subject to the liquidation waterfall of the Charter — i.e., consideration is distributed “as if the Sale of the Company were a Liquidation Event.” Section 3.2(d) limits stockholder representations and warranties to title, authority, and enforceability, and limits indemnification to a several (not joint) basis capped at the consideration received; no stockholder may be required to enter non-competition or non-solicitation covenants. These terms are favorable to the selling stockholders and would, if the drag-along is invoked, govern the stockholders’ obligations in the merger agreement.
109	
110	V. AREA 3 — IMPEDIMENTS TO CLOSING A REVERSE TRIANGULAR MERGER
111	A. Defined Terms Clearly Capture the Structure
112	The reverse triangular merger is unambiguously captured by the operative defined terms:
113	“Liquidation Event” (Charter § 4.3.2(d)(i)): “any merger, consolidation, or other business combination of the Corporation with or into another entity (whether or not the Corporation is the surviving entity), other than [one in which the stockholders retain at least 50% of the voting power].” The explicit “whether or not the Corporation is the surviving entity” language confirms that both forward and reverse mergers are captured. Because Terravox stockholders will retain 0% of the voting power post-merger (Hargrove will own 100%), the Transaction is a Liquidation Event. Section 4.3.2(d)(iii) (transfer of more than 50% of voting power) is independently satisfied.
114	“Sale of the Company” (Stockholders’ Agreement § 1.1; Voting Agreement § 1): “any merger, consolidation, or reorganization of the Company in which the stockholders of the Company immediately prior to such transaction do not retain a majority of the voting power of the surviving or resulting entity.” The Transaction satisfies this definition.
115	Accordingly, there is no structural ambiguity as to whether the reverse triangular merger triggers the Liquidation Event and Sale of the Company definitions. The consent and termination consequences follow.
116	B. DGCL Section 251(f) Does Not Apply
117	As noted in Section III, because all Terravox shares will be cancelled and converted into cash, the Section 251(f) exemption from stockholder approval is unavailable, and a full stockholder vote under Section 251(d) is required. This is consistent with the structure (a 100% cash-out acquisition) and with Company counsel’s analysis.
118	C. Survival of the Company and Its Contracts
119	In a reverse triangular merger, Terravox survives as a wholly-owned subsidiary. This has two principal implications:
120	Contracts generally survive without assignment. Because the legal entity persists, contracts that would otherwise require counterparty consent upon an assignment or change of control by merger may not be triggered (depending on the specific change-of-control language in each contract). This is generally an advantage of the reverse triangular structure. However, certain contracts contain change-of-control triggers that apply regardless of entity survival — most notably the Clearfield term loan (see Section IX) and potentially the Permian Basin Energy Co. master services agreement and other commercial contracts. We have not yet reviewed these contracts and cannot confirm whether change-of-control consents are required.
121	Charter and Bylaws survive (as subsidiary documents). The Charter and Bylaws remain in effect post-closing until amended. The protective provisions become practically moot because no Preferred Stock will remain outstanding post-closing, but they must be satisfied at closing (before the shares are cancelled). The Series C separate-class consent is therefore a closing condition, not a post-closing concern.
122	D. The RRA “Deemed Liquidation Event” Gap
123	The RRA terminates upon “a Deemed Liquidation Event” (Section 9(c)), but the RRA does not define “Deemed Liquidation Event.” The Charter defines “Liquidation Event” (not “Deemed Liquidation Event”), and the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement define “Sale of the Company.” The absence of a defined term in the RRA creates ambiguity as to whether the Transaction terminates the RRA. This is addressed in detail in Sections VIII (Area 6) and XI (Area 10). The merger agreement should include an express termination/waiver of the RRA at closing to eliminate this ambiguity.
124	E. Conclusion on Structure
125	The reverse triangular merger structure does not create unique definitional impediments — the operative defined terms clearly capture it. The principal structure-related risks are (i) the survival of the Company’s contracts (requiring review for change-of-control consents, particularly the Clearfield loan) and (ii) the RRA definitional gap. Neither is a deal-breaker, but both require affirmative attention in the merger agreement and diligence.
126	
127	VI. AREA 4 — TRANSFER RESTRICTIONS
128	A. ROFR and Co-Sale (Stockholders’ Agreement §§ 2.2–2.3)
129	Section 2.1 prohibits any Restricted Stockholder from Transferring Shares except (a) in compliance with Article 2, (b) pursuant to a Permitted Transfer (Section 2.7), or (c) “in connection with a Sale of the Company consummated in accordance with Article 3.” Section 2.7(e) lists as a Permitted Transfer any “Transfer[] in connection with a Sale of the Company consummated in accordance with Article 3.”
130	The right of first refusal (Section 2.2) is triggered when a Restricted Stockholder “receives a bona fide written offer from a third party … to purchase any Shares and desires to accept such offer.” The co-sale right (Section 2.3) is triggered when the Company and other Restricted Stockholders do not purchase all Shares subject to a ROFR Notice. These provisions are designed for individual stockholder sales to third parties, not for a statutory merger in which all shares are cancelled and converted by operation of law.
131	Whether the merger triggers ROFR/co-sale turns on the conditional carve-out. The Sale of the Company carve-out in Sections 2.1(c) and 2.7(e) is conditioned on the Sale of the Company being “consummated in accordance with Article 3” — i.e., pursuant to the drag-along. If the drag-along is invoked and the Sale of the Company is consummated under Article 3, the merger is a Permitted Transfer and ROFR/co-sale do not apply. If the drag-along is not invoked (for example, because the Transaction proceeds on the basis of stockholder consents and a stockholder vote without a formal drag-along trigger), the conditional language creates ambiguity as to whether the merger falls within the carve-out.
132	Practical resolution. The Stockholders’ Agreement terminates upon “a Sale of the Company” (Section 6.1(b)), so the transfer restrictions cease upon closing in any event. The concern is whether the restrictions impede the path to closing. Because the merger is a statutory transaction (not a voluntary sale by an individual stockholder) and the Stockholders’ Agreement terminates upon the Sale of the Company, the better view is that ROFR/co-sale do not apply to the merger. Nonetheless, the conditional carve-out language is imprecise.
133	Recommendation. Obtain an interpretive confirmation or waiver from the stockholder parties (or structure the Transaction to invoke the drag-along formally) to eliminate any argument that ROFR or co-sale rights are triggered. The merger agreement should include a representation that the Transaction constitutes a Sale of the Company and that the transfer restrictions do not apply.
134	B. Tag-Along Rights (Section 2.4)
135	Tag-along rights apply to a “Founder Transfer” — a Founder’s transfer of more than 5% of the outstanding Shares to a non-Affiliate third party. The merger is not a Founder Transfer in the ordinary sense; it is a Sale of the Company. The same analysis as ROFR/co-sale applies: the Sale of the Company carve-out and the termination of the Stockholders’ Agreement upon a Sale of the Company (Section 6.1(b)) should govern. No separate impediment is identified, but the conditional-carve-out ambiguity noted above applies equally here.
136	C. Founder Lock-Up (Section 2.5)
137	The 12-month lock-up period ran from November 3, 2021 and expired on November 3, 2022. It is no longer in effect and presents no impediment.
138	D. Voting Agreement Transfer Restriction (Section 6.2)
139	Section 6.2 of the Voting Agreement prohibits Transfers unless the transferee agrees to be bound by the Voting Agreement. The Voting Agreement terminates upon a Sale of the Company (Section 7.1(b)), so this restriction ceases upon closing. The merger is not a voluntary Transfer by a party in the conventional sense. No impediment is identified.
140	E. Conclusion on Transfer Restrictions
141	The transfer restrictions should not impede the Transaction, particularly given that the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement both terminate upon a Sale of the Company. The principal risk is the conditional carve-out language (“consummated in accordance with Article 3”), which creates ambiguity if the drag-along is not formally invoked. We recommend addressing this through waiver/confirmation or formal drag-along invocation, and through appropriate provisions in the merger agreement.
142	
143	VII. AREA 5 — BOARD COMPOSITION: POST-CLOSING IMPLICATIONS
144	A. Termination of the Voting Agreement Upon the Merger
145	Section 7.1 of the Voting Agreement provides that the agreement terminates upon the earliest of: (a) an IPO; (b) “the consummation of a Sale of the Company (as defined in Section 1)”; (c) written consent of the Company and specified stockholder majorities; or (d) November 3, 2026 (the fifth anniversary).
146	The “Sale of the Company” definition (Section 1) captures the reverse triangular merger (a merger in which the stockholders do not retain a majority of the voting power of the surviving entity). Accordingly, the Voting Agreement terminates upon closing of the Transaction. The board designation rights in Section 2.2 lapse at closing; they do not survive, and the November 3, 2026 sunset is moot if the Transaction closes before that date (target Q1 2025).
147	Post-closing board. Upon closing, Hargrove, as the sole stockholder of the surviving subsidiary, will control the board and may appoint directors as it sees fit. No designation rights, voting obligations, or committee-composition requirements under the Voting Agreement survive. This is a clean outcome.
148	B. Stonemill’s Board Observer Right (Section 4)
149	Section 4.1 grants Stonemill the right to appoint a non-voting observer to attend all Board (and committee) meetings and to receive all Board materials, for so long as Stonemill holds at least 1,000,000 shares of Series C Preferred Stock (or Common issued upon conversion). Section 7.3 provides that the observer right terminates if Stonemill ceases to hold the minimum share threshold.
150	Upon closing, all Series C Preferred Stock is cancelled and converted into cash, so Stonemill ceases to hold any Series C (or Common issued upon conversion). The observer right therefore terminates at closing. In addition, the Voting Agreement itself terminates upon the Sale of the Company (Section 7.1(b)), independently terminating the observer right. The observer right does not survive the Transaction.
151	During the signing-to-closing period, however, the observer right is live. Stonemill’s observer is entitled to attend Board meetings and receive Board materials during the pendency of the Transaction. This is significant because the Board will be considering the merger agreement and related matters. Section 4.2 permits the Company to exclude the observer (and withhold materials) to preserve attorney-client privilege or to address a bona fide conflict of interest between Stonemill and the Company, upon advice of counsel. Given Stonemill’s adversarial posture, the Company should be prepared to invoke this exclusion judiciously for privileged deliberations and conflict-sensitive matters. We note that the Stonemill observer was not present at the April 17, 2024 meeting at which the Board authorized engagement with Hargrove — a fact relevant to the sale-process analysis in Section X (Area 9).
152	C. Committee Composition (Section 2.4)
153	Section 2.4 provides that, unless otherwise agreed by the Investor Stockholders, each Board committee must include at least one Investor Stockholder designee. This requirement is live during the signing-to-closing period. The Special Committee currently includes Sandra Okafor (Northbridge’s designee), satisfying this requirement. Post-closing, the requirement is moot. The composition of the Special Committee is analyzed in Section X (Area 9).
154	D. Conclusion on Board Composition
155	The Voting Agreement and its designation rights and observer rights terminate cleanly upon closing. No post-closing governance entanglement arises from the Voting Agreement. The principal operational concern is managing Stonemill’s observer rights (and information access) during the signing-to-closing period.

157	VIII. AREA 6 — CROSS-DOCUMENT DEFINITIONAL INCONSISTISTENCIES
158	We compared the key defined terms across all five governance documents. The most significant findings are as follows.
159	A. “Deemed Liquidation Event” (RRA) Is Undefined — Critical Gap
160	The RRA terminates upon “a Deemed Liquidation Event” (Section 9(c)) but does not define “Deemed Liquidation Event” anywhere in the agreement (the defined terms in Section 1 do not include it). The Charter defines “Liquidation Event” (Section 4.3.2(d)); the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement define “Sale of the Company” (each in Section 1). The RRA does not cross-reference the Charter’s “Liquidation Event” definition.
161	The evident intent is that “Deemed Liquidation Event” corresponds to the Charter’s “Liquidation Event” (which captures the merger). However, because the term is undefined, a strict reading creates ambiguity as to whether the Transaction terminates the RRA. This is the most significant cross-document inconsistency and has direct consequences for the RRA’s post-transaction treatment (Section XI, Area 10). Severity: Significant. The merger agreement should include an express termination/waiver of the RRA at closing.
162	B. Threshold Mismatch: “At Least 50%” (Charter) vs. “Majority” (Stockholders’ Agreement / Voting Agreement)
163	Charter § 4.3.2(d)(i): a merger is a Liquidation Event unless the Company’s stockholders “continue to hold at least fifty percent (50%)” of the voting power of the surviving entity.
164	Stockholders’ Agreement § 1.1 / Voting Agreement § 1: a merger is a Sale of the Company if the stockholders “do not retain a majority” of the voting power of the surviving entity.
165	“At least 50%” (≥50%) and “majority” (>50%) differ at the boundary: if the stockholders retained exactly 50% of the voting power, the transaction would not be a Liquidation Event under the Charter (50% ≥ 50%) but would be a Sale of the Company under the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement (50% is not a majority, so the stockholders do not “retain a majority”). For the Hargrove Transaction (stockholders retain 0%), both definitions are triggered, so there is no practical effect here. The inconsistency is nonetheless a drafting defect that could produce divergent outcomes in other transactions (e.g., a minority investment or a partial roll-over structure). Severity: Moderate.
166	C. Equity Financing Carve-Out Present in Charter, Absent Elsewhere
167	Charter § 4.3.2(d)(iii): excludes from the Liquidation Event definition “an equity financing transaction primarily for capital-raising purposes.”
168	Stockholders’ Agreement § 1.1 / Voting Agreement § 1: contain no equivalent equity-financing carve-out in their Sale of the Company definitions.
169	Accordingly, an equity financing that results in the transfer of more than 50% of the voting power would be a Sale of the Company (triggering drag-along, termination, etc.) under the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement but would not be a Liquidation Event under the Charter (and thus would not trigger the liquidation preference or the protective provisions). This divergence is not implicated by the Hargrove Transaction (which is not an equity financing) but is a latent inconsistency. Severity: Moderate.
170	D. IPO Definition Inconsistencies
171	Charter § 4.3.3(b)(i): mandatory conversion upon an IPO with aggregate gross proceeds of at least $75,000,000 and a price per share of at least $55.50 (3x the Series C Original Issue Price).
172	Stockholders’ Agreement § 6.1(a): termination upon an IPO with gross proceeds of at least $75,000,000 (no per-share price threshold).
173	Voting Agreement § 7.1(a): termination upon an IPO with gross proceeds of at least $75,000,000 (no per-share price threshold).
174	RRA § 1: “IPO” means the Company’s first underwritten public offering of Common Stock (no proceeds threshold).
175	The Charter’s per-share price threshold ($55.50) is unique to the Charter and affects only the timing of mandatory conversion of Preferred Stock. The divergence is not implicated by the Transaction but is a latent inconsistency affecting when the various agreements would terminate in an IPO scenario. Severity: Moderate.
176	E. Other Minor Inconsistencies
177	The Charter’s Liquidation Event asset-disposition prong (Section 4.3.2(d)(ii)) uses “sale, lease, transfer, exclusive license, or other disposition,” while the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement use “sale, lease, exclusive license, or other disposition” (omitting “transfer”). Substantively similar; minor drafting variance.
178	The Charter uses “merger, consolidation, or other business combination” (Section 4.3.2(d)(i)); the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement use “merger, consolidation, or reorganization.” Substantively similar; minor drafting variance.
179	“Affiliate” is defined only in the Stockholders’ Agreement (Section 1.1); the Voting Agreement and RRA use the concept without defining it.
180	F. Document Completeness Question
181	The preamble to the Board Minutes refers to “the Investors’ Rights Agreement, the Voting Agreement, the Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement, and the Stockholders’ Agreement (collectively, the ‘Governance Agreements’)” — listing four agreements. We have received three (the Stockholders’ Agreement, the Voting Agreement, and the RRA). It appears that the Stockholders’ Agreement combines the ROFR/co-sale and information-rights functions (Articles 2 and 4) and the RRA provides registration rights, such that the “Investors’ Rights Agreement” and “Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement” referenced in the minutes may be informal names for the documents we have. We should confirm with Company counsel that we have received all operative governance agreements and that no separate Investors’ Rights Agreement or ROFR/Co-Sale Agreement exists. Severity: Moderate (document completeness).

222	Methodology: Series C takes its $18.50/share liquidation preference ($144.3 million) because converting would yield only approximately $13.95/share on a pro rata basis across all 33,900,000 fully diluted shares — less than the preference. The remaining approximately $328.7 million is shared pro rata among Series B, Series A, and Common (26,100,000 shares), yielding approximately $12.59/share. Series B and Series A each convert because $12.59/share exceeds their respective preferences ($9.25 and $4.00).
223	This analysis reveals two important points:
224	The September 25, 2024 Board minutes’ characterization appears imprecise. The minutes state that “all holders of Preferred Stock would receive amounts in excess of their respective liquidation preferences on an as-converted basis, suggesting that participation on an as-converted basis would be economically favorable for all preferred holders.” In fact, Series C does not receive an amount in excess of its liquidation preference — it receives exactly its preference ($18.50/share) and does not participate on an as-converted basis (because as-converted participation would yield less). Only Series B and Series A receive amounts in excess of their preferences. This imprecision in the Board’s characterization should be noted; it may reflect an oversimplification in the minutes or in Meridian’s preliminary analysis, and it is relevant to the fairness analysis (Section X, Area 9).
225	Stonemill’s economic position explains its resistance. At $485 million enterprise value, Stonemill receives approximately $144.3 million — essentially a return of its Original Issue Price ($18.50 × 7,800,000), with no gain. Meanwhile, Northbridge, Ridgeline, and the Common holders capture the upside above the preferences. This is the economic root of Stonemill’s opposition: the Series C liquidation preference is so large relative to the equity value that Stonemill is, in effect, the “last money in” bearing the risk with no upside at this price. Stonemill’s likely objective — a higher allocation of consideration or a side arrangement — is economically rational from its perspective and should inform the negotiation strategy.
226	D. Impact on Equity Awards
227	Malhotra options (350,000 @ $14.30): The implied Common per-share consideration (~$12.59) is below the $14.30 exercise price, meaning these options are currently underwater. If the options are cashed out at closing on a single-trigger basis, the net value would be zero (or the options would be cancelled for no consideration). However, this depends on the final per-share consideration (which depends on the final equity value and waterfall) and the Plan’s treatment of underwater options. If the deal value increases, the options could move into the money.
228	Yeung RSUs (200,000): RSUs have no exercise price. If single-trigger accelerated, 200,000 RSUs × ~$12.59/share ≈ $2.5 million. As of a Q1 2025 closing, only approximately one-third would be vested absent acceleration.
229	Aggregate impact: The aggregate acceleration cost appears modest based on the identified awards, but the full option pool (a portion of the 10,100,000 shares) is unquantified. The Plan and complete award schedule are required to assess the total impact.
230	E. Equity Plan Share Reserve Increase (July 2023) — Stockholder Approval Question
231	At the July 19, 2023 Board meeting, the Board approved an amendment to the Plan increasing the share reserve from 8,000,000 to 9,500,000 shares (a 1,500,000-share increase). The minutes reflect that outside counsel (Mr. Rourke) “noted that the amendment required Board approval,” and the Board approved the increase 7-0. The minutes do not reflect any stockholder approval.
232	Whether stockholder approval was required depends on the Plan’s terms (which we have not received). Under Delaware law, increasing a plan’s share reserve does not increase the authorized capital stock (and thus does not trigger the Charter’s Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) protective provision, which concerns authorized shares). However, stockholder approval may be required (i) if the Plan so provides, (ii) to maintain incentive stock option (“ISO”) qualification under Section 422 of the Internal Revenue Code (if the Plan grants ISOs), or (iii) for other tax or securities-law reasons. As a private company, exchange-listing standards (e.g., the 20% rule) do not apply. We must review the Plan to determine whether stockholder approval was required and, if so, whether it was obtained. Severity: Moderate (pending Plan review).
233	The anti-dilution provisions of the Charter (Section 4.3.4(b)(i)) exempt shares issued “pursuant to equity incentive plans or stock purchase plans approved by the Board of Directors, including at least one director elected by the holders of Preferred Stock.” The Board includes Preferred-designated directors (Messrs. Kleinhans and Trask and Ms. Okafor), so grants under the Plan are exempt from anti-dilution adjustment. No anti-dilution issue is presented by the reserve increase or the grants.
234	
235	X. AREA 8 — AUTHORIZATION OF CORPORATE ACTIONS: BOARD MINUTES REVIEW
236	We reviewed all eight sets of Board minutes (January 18, 2023 through September 25, 2024). For each significant corporate action, we assessed whether proper Board approval was obtained and whether stockholder or Preferred Stockholder consent was required and, if so, whether it was obtained.
237	A. Meeting-by-Meeting Summary
238	Meeting 1 — January 18, 2023 (7/7 present; quorum satisfied). Approved the FY2023 operating budget; authorized the Permian Basin Energy Co. master services agreement (up to $15 million); and authorized the engagement of Meridian Partners LLC as financial advisor to explore strategic alternatives. All actions approved 7-0. No protective provision implicated. Properly authorized.
