101:The Board must adopt a resolution approving the merger agreement and the merger (DGCL § 251(b)). Under Bylaws Section 3.7, a majority of the total number of directors then in office (4 of 7) constitutes a quorum, and the act of a majority of directors present at a quorum is the act of the Board. The Board has authorized management to negotiate definitive documentation (Board Minutes, Sept. 25, 2024, approved 6-1, Trask dissenting), but final Board approval of the signed merger agreement has not yet been obtained. 115:We note for completeness that several other Section 4.3.6(a) protective provisions are not directly triggered by the merger itself but are relevant to related actions: Section 4.3.6(a)(i) (charter/bylaw amendments adversely affecting Preferred — relevant if the merger agreement requires a charter amendment); Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) (increasing/decreasing authorized shares); and Section 4.3.6(a)(vii) (increasing/decreasing Board size). The merger as structured (cancellation of all equity for cash) does not require a charter amendment, so these are not independently triggered, but we should confirm that the merger agreement does not contemplate any pre-closing charter amendment that would independently require Preferred consent.
253:Charter protective provisions. Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) requires Preferred consent to “increase or decrease the total number of authorized shares of Common Stock or Preferred Stock, or any series thereof.” The share-reserve increase does not increase the authorized Common Stock (50,000,000 authorized, unchanged); it merely reserves additional authorized shares for issuance under the Plan. Accordingly, Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) is likely not triggered. However, the anti-dilution exception in Section 4.3.4(b)(i) exempts options issued under a Board-approved plan (including at least one Preferred-designated director) from anti-dilution adjustment, so the increase should not trigger Preferred anti-dilution. We nonetheless recommend confirming that no other protective provision (e.g., a plan-specific consent right in a separate Investors’ Rights Agreement, if one exists — see Section VIII.C) was implicated.
269:January 24, 2024: The Board authorized the CEO to negotiate and execute a non-binding LOI for a potential acquisition of SensorLink for up to $22,000,000 (7-0).
281:Strategic alternatives process (Jan. 2023 – present): The Board authorized engagement of Meridian Partners (Jan. 18, 2023), formed the Special Committee (April 12, 2023), pursued exclusivity with “Beta” (July 19, 2023, approved 6-1, Trask dissenting — Trask objected the process was “insufficiently broad”), terminated Beta exclusivity (Oct. 11, 2023), received inbound Hargrove interest (April 17, 2024), granted Hargrove exclusivity (July 10, 2024, 6-1, Trask dissenting), and authorized definitive-documentation negotiation (Sept. 25, 2024, 6-1, Trask dissenting). Board approvals were properly obtained at each step with quorums present. The process narrative is relevant to the fiduciary-duty analysis in Section XI.

Note that the Yeung RSU grant was approved at the April 17, 2024 meeting at which only 5 of 7 directors were present (Trask and Garrett absent) and at which the Yeung RSU was recommended by the Compensation Committee chaired by Lau; Yeung is the CFO and recording secretary. While Yeung is not a director (so no direct Board conflict), the grant to the CFO at the same meeting that authorized the Hargrove engagement is worth noting for process-completeness; it does not appear improper on its face.

Trask was absent (he notified the Chairman on April 15 of a scheduling conflict and provided no proxy). Garrett was also absent (family medical). A quorum (4) was present, and the resolution passed 5-0, so the action was validly taken as a matter of corporate law.

229:The risk. Because the term is undefined, a holder could argue that the RRA’s termination trigger is ambiguous and that registration rights survive the merger. In an all-cash merger, the practical impact is limited because post-closing there are no “Registrable Securities” outstanding (the Preferred is cancelled for cash; no Common is issued upon conversion), so the registration rights would be largely moot. However, the definitional gap (i) creates avoidable ambiguity, (ii) would become material if the deal structure ever included stock consideration, and (iii) leaves the RRA’s indemnification provisions (Section 7, which survive termination under Section 9) technically in play.
252:Plan-required stockholder approval. Many VC-backed equity incentive plans require stockholder approval for amendments that increase the share reserve. 214:C. Stonemill’s board observer right — does it survive?
215:Section 4.1 grants Stonemill the right to appoint a non-voting board observer for so long as Stonemill holds at least 1,000,000 shares of Series C (or Common issued upon conversion). The observer is entitled to attend all Board and committee meetings and to receive all materials provided to directors (subject to attorney-client privilege and conflict-of-interest exclusions under Section 4.2).
216:The observer right does not survive the merger. Two independent reasons: (1) the Voting Agreement terminates at closing under Section 7.1(b) (Sale of the Company); and (2) even if the Voting Agreement did not terminate, the observer right is conditioned on Stonemill holding ≥1,000,000 Series C (or conversion Common), and at closing Stonemill’s Series C is cancelled for cash — so the condition fails and the right expires under Section 7.3. Post-closing, Hargrove controls the Terravox board and there is no continuing observer right.
218:If, contrary to expectation, the merger were held not to be a “Sale of the Company” under the Voting Agreement (e.g., due to a definitional dispute), the Voting Agreement would not terminate at closing and would continue in force until the November 3, 2026 sunset. In that scenario, the designation rights and voting obligations would technically remain in force. However, the practical impact is limited: post-closing, the former stockholders (Founders and Investors) no longer hold shares (cashed out), so their voting obligations are moot, and the individual designation/observer rights expire under Section 7.3 when the minimum-share thresholds are no longer met. The residual risk is low but non-zero, and it reinforces the importance of the definitional-consistency analysis in Section VIII. We recommend that the merger agreement include an express termination/waiver of the Voting Agreement at closing to eliminate any sunset-related lingering risk.
295:The Stonemill board observer was also absent from the April 17, 2024 meeting. The Voting Agreement (Section 4.1) entitles the Stonemill observer to attend all Board meetings and to receive all materials provided to directors. If the observer was not given proper notice or was not provided the meeting materials, Stonemill could claim a breach of the observer right, reinforcing its narrative that it was excluded from the sale process at a critical juncture. The Minutes do not state whether the observer was notified; this should be confirmed.
297:Assessment. The April 17 action was legally valid (quorum + majority), so Trask’s absence does not invalidate the authorization. However, the combination of (i) Trask’s absence, (ii) the observer’s absence, (iii) Trask’s prompt written objection, and (iv) the interested-director taint on the Special Committee creates a procedural narrative that Stonemill can exploit to allege that the sale process was conducted without full stockholder/director participation and without a fully independent committee. This does not rise to a deal-breaker, but it increases litigation risk and should be mitigated.
299:Cure the observer issue: confirm whether the Stonemill observer was notified of and provided materials for the April 17, 2024 meeting; if not, document the reason and ensure full observer compliance going forward (notice + materials for all future meetings). A breach, if any, should be cured prospectively.

230	E. Equity Plan Share Reserve Increase (July 2023) — Stockholder Approval Question
231	At the July 19, 2023 Board meeting, the Board approved an amendment to the Plan increasing the share reserve from 8,000,000 to 9,500,000 shares (a 1,500,000-share increase). The minutes reflect that outside counsel (Mr. Rourke) “noted that the amendment required Board approval,” and the Board approved the increase 7-0. The minutes do not reflect any stockholder approval.
232	Whether stockholder approval was required depends on the Plan’s terms (which we have not received). Under Delaware law, increasing a plan’s share reserve does not increase the authorized capital stock (and thus does not trigger the Charter’s Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) protective provision, which concerns authorized shares). However, stockholder approval may be required (i) if the Plan so provides, (ii) to maintain incentive stock option (“ISO”) qualification under Section 422 of the Internal Revenue Code (if the Plan grants ISOs), or (iii) for other tax or securities-law reasons. As a private company, exchange-listing standards (e.g., the 20% rule) do not apply. We must review the Plan to determine whether stockholder approval was required and, if so, whether it was obtained. Severity: Moderate (pending Plan review).
233	The anti-dilution provisions of the Charter (Section 4.3.4(b)(i)) exempt shares issued “pursuant to equity incentive plans or stock purchase plans approved by the Board of Directors, including at least one director elected by the holders of Preferred Stock.” The Board includes Preferred-designated directors (Messrs. Kleinhans and Trask and Ms. Okafor), so grants under the Plan are exempt from anti-dilution adjustment. No anti-dilution issue is presented by the reserve increase or the grants.
234	
235	X. AREA 8 — AUTHORIZATION OF CORPORATE ACTIONS: BOARD MINUTES REVIEW
236	We reviewed all eight sets of Board minutes (January 18, 2023 through September 25, 2024). For each significant corporate action, we assessed whether proper Board approval was obtained and whether stockholder or Preferred Stockholder consent was required and, if so, whether it was obtained.

############ C-038 WRITTEN CONSENT mechanism ############
--- iter0 ---
12:The central finding of this diligence is that Stonemill Capital Partners Fund VI, LP (“Stonemill”) holds a hard, charter-level blocking right over the Transaction. As the holder of 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, Stonemill alone controls the Series C separate-class protective provision under Section 4.3.6(b)(i) of the Restated Certificate, which requires the affirmative vote or written consent of holders of at least 66⅔% of the Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class, to effect any “Liquidation Event.” The reverse triangular merger is unambiguously a “Liquidation Event.” Because Stonemill holds all 7,800,000 outstanding Series C shares, the 66⅔% threshold (≥5,200,000 shares) cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent. This blocking right cannot be cured by the drag-along mechanism in the Stockholders’ Agreement with any certainty under Delaware law, as discussed in Section IV.
103:DGCL § 251(c) requires the approval of holders of a majority of the outstanding shares of stock entitled to vote (the Company’s Restated Certificate and the protective provisions cause the Preferred to vote on an as-converted basis with the Common as a single class on this matter). This is a majority of the approximately 33,900,000 fully diluted shares, i.e., more than 16,950,000 shares. Status: Not yet obtained. This vote can be taken by written consent in lieu of a meeting under DGCL § 228 and Restated Certificate Article VI, Section 6.2 / Bylaws Section 2.6.
105:So long as any Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least a majority (greater than 50%) of the then-outstanding Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis (Section 4.3.6(a)(vi)). The Restated Certificate specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of more than 8,250,000 shares of Preferred (based on 16,500,000 Preferred shares outstanding: 3,500,000 Series A; 5,200,000 Series B; 7,800,000 Series C).
108:So long as any Series C Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class (Section 4.3.6(b)(i)). This consent is “separate from and in addition to” the all-Preferred consent under Section 4.3.6(a). The Restated Certificate specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of at least 5,200,000 shares of Series C (based on 7,800,000 Series C shares outstanding).
113:We considered whether the merger could be structured to avoid a stockholder vote under DGCL § 251(f) (no stockholder vote required where, among other things, no amendment to the certificate of incorporation is made in the merger and either the acquirer owns 90% of the target or certain other conditions are met). This is not available here: Hargrove does not currently own any Terravox stock, and the merger will cancel all outstanding equity for consideration. A stockholder vote (or written consent) is required.
147:This is the decisive question for the Transaction. Even if the drag-along is triggered without Stonemill and compels Stonemill to vote its Series C in favor, the question is whether that compelled vote satisfies the charter’s separate-class protective provision in Section 4.3.6(b)(i), which requires the “affirmative vote or written consent” of 66⅔% of the Series C voting as a separate class.
149:The charter is satisfied by a vote, not by a discretionary veto. Section 4.3.6(b) is satisfied by “the prior written consent or affirmative vote” of 66⅔% of the Series C. It does not require a voluntary or uncoerced consent; it requires a vote or consent of the requisite threshold. If the drag-along compels Stonemill (100% of the Series C) to vote in favor, that is an “affirmative vote” of 100% of the Series C, which exceeds the 66⅔% threshold. On this reading, the charter requirement is satisfied, not waived or amended — the protective provision is given effect through a compelled vote.
212:Section 7.1 provides that the Voting Agreement terminates upon the earliest of: (a) an IPO; (b) the consummation of a Sale of the Company (as defined in Section 1); (c) written consent of the Company, a majority of the Founder Common, and a majority of each series of Preferred held by parties (voting as separate classes); or (d) November 3, 2026 (the fifth anniversary — the “sunset”).
266:Recommendation. Request the stockholder consent records (written consents) for the period around October 2023 to confirm whether Preferred consent was obtained. If not, assess cure options (ratification) and disclose in the merger agreement reps.
308:Registration rights terminate with respect to a particular Holder upon the earliest of: (a) the Holder being able to sell all its Registrable Securities in a single three-month period under Rule 144 without volume/manner-of-sale restrictions and without the current-public-information requirement; (b) the fifth anniversary of the IPO closing; or (c) “a Deemed Liquidation Event.” The Agreement terminates in its entirety upon the earliest of (i) termination as to all Holders under (a)/(b)/(c), or (ii) the written consent of the Company and Holders of a majority of the then-outstanding Registrable Securities.

D. Share-reserve increase — stockholder approval (Significant)
The Plan share reserve was increased from 8,000,000 to 9,500,000 shares on July 19, 2023, by Board resolution only (approved 7-0). Two questions arise:
Plan-required stockholder approval. Many VC-backed equity incentive plans require stockholder approval for amendments that increase the share reserve. We cannot confirm whether the Plan requires stockholder approval without reviewing the Plan. If the Plan requires stockholder approval and it was not obtained, the amendment may be invalid and awards granted under the increased reserve may be vulnerable.
Charter protective provisions. Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) requires Preferred consent to “increase or decrease the total number of authorized shares of Common Stock or Preferred Stock, or any series thereof.” The share-reserve increase does not increase the authorized Common Stock (50,000,000 authorized, unchanged); it merely reserves additional authorized shares for issuance under the Plan. Accordingly, Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) is likely not triggered. However, the anti-dilution exception in Section 4.3.4(b)(i) exempts options issued under a Board-approved plan (including at least one Preferred-designated director) from anti-dilution adjustment, so the increase should not trigger Preferred anti-dilution. We nonetheless recommend confirming that no other protective provision (e.g., a plan-specific consent right in a separate Investors’ Rights Agreement, if one exists — see Section VIII.C) was implicated.
Recommendation. Request the Plan (as amended), the amendment effecting the share-reserve increase, and any stockholder consent records relating to the increase. Confirm whether stockholder approval was required and obtained; if not, assess the validity of the increase and consider a ratification/curative consent.

Effort visibly shifted *toward* cross-referencing and issue-mapping and *away* from the foundational cap-table/charter recitals.

### Q4 — Bottom-line mechanism

The "complete issue census + cross-reference every document" prose caused a **displacement/crowding-out** regression, not over-coverage. It pushed the agent to restructure the memo around an exhaustive issue-by-issue and document-by-document taxonomy (10 "AREAS," a consolidated leverage map, meeting-by-meeting minutes review). That reorganization **deleted the plain foundational recitals** — the dedicated capitalization/charter section that, in iter0, simply stated the authorized share counts (C-001/2/3), summed the aggregate liquidation preference (C-008), and labeled the preference "1x non-participating" (C-009). Those are easy, must-state baseline facts that an issue-spotting lens treats as uninteresting and skips. The same lens also **over-resolved** open questions into clean conclusions (observer right "terminates cleanly," killing the C-030 survival argument) and **mis-routed cures** (it recommended a ratifying consent for the debt issue but not for the equity-plan issue, and reframed the equity-plan defect as a neutral "question" rather than a validity risk — losing C-021/C-022). Net: the prose optimized for breadth of *issues* while silently dropping breadth of *required baseline facts and recommendations* — a dilution failure where chasing comprehensive cross-referencing cost eight gimme criteria that iter0 banked by just stating the numbers. The lesson: a "cover everything" instruction must explicitly protect the mandatory foundational recitals (full authorized/outstanding cap table, aggregate figures, preference characterization) and require an explicit remedial recommendation per defect, or restructuring will quietly evict them.
